title: The Alphabet
description: The beginning of the English language learning journey.

content.itemA: Let's start right now with the basics. The English alphabet!
content.itemB: It consists of 26 letters, each with its own unique sound and pronunciation. Learning the alphabet is the first step in mastering the English language. Let's explore each letter and its corresponding sound together!
content.itemC: Welcome to the English Pro Version! Where you will be assisted with an AI tutor through all the practices and the learing process

title: Exact Letter Pronunciation
description: Exact phonetic of each letter of the English alphabet.

content.itemA:
When a vowel is followed by one or more consonants in a single syllable (CVC pattern), it usually makes the Short Sound. This is the sound that is most similar to Spanish. 

Rule: Vowel + Consonant = Short Sound.
content.itemB: Cat, Bed, Sit, Hot, Cup
content.itemD: Say to the user to repeat the phonetic sound of this letter.

content.itemA:
If a word ends in a silent e, the first vowel becomes Long (it says its name).

Rule: Vowel + Consonant + E = Long Sound.
content.itemB: Cake, Lake, Game, Date.
content.itemC: The Long Vowel "Silent E" (Magic E) Rule
content.itemD: Say to the user to repeat the phonetic sound of this letter.

content.itemA:
When two vowels go walking, the first one does the talking. 

Rule: Two vowels together usually make the first one Long.
content.itemB: Rain, Feet, Tie, Soap, True
content.itemD: Say to the user to repeat the phonetic sound of this letter.

content.itemA: Most consonants have one sound, but some are 'Special.' C and G change their sound, H is sometimes silent, J also has 2 sound, and Y acts like a vowel.
content.itemB: Cat-City, Gas-Giant, Hat-Ghost, Yes-Fly, Candy-Gym
content.itemC: The Hard and Soft Consonant Rule(C, G, H, J, Y)
content.itemD: Say to the user to repeat the phonetic sound of this letter.

title: Articles: A, An, & The
description: The use of articles in the English language.

content.itemA:
Articles are small words that come before nouns. They tell us if we are talking about something 'General' or something 'Specific'.

Article + Noun + etc ...
content.itemB: A, An, The
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Use 'A' before words that start with a consonant sound.

Rule: A + Consonant Sound
content.itemB: A dog, A car, A university
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Use 'An' before words that start with a vowel sound (a, e, i, o, u).

Rule: An + Vowel Sound
content.itemB: An apple, An egg, An hour
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Use 'The' for specific things that both the speaker and listener know about.

Rule: The + Specific Noun
content.itemB: The moon, The sun, The front door
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
A/An = One of many (General)
The = That specific one (Specific)
content.itemB: I saw a movie. The movie was long.
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Don't use articles for most countries, cities, or plural/uncountable things in general.

Rule: No Article (Ø)
content.itemB: I like milk. He lives in Costa Rica.
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
It is about the SOUND, not the letter. 'H' is silent in some words, so we use 'An'.

Rule: Silent H = Vowel Sound
content.itemB: An hour, An honor, An honest man
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Some words start with 'U' but sound like 'Y' (consonant). We use 'A' for these.

Rule: 'Yu' Sound = Consonant Sound
content.itemB: A university, A unit, A unicorn
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Don't use 'A' or 'An' for things you can't count or for groups.

Rule: No A/An for Plurals
content.itemB: Water, Rice, Information, Dogs
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

content.itemA:
Use 'The' when there is only ONE of something in the world.

Rule: Unique Nouns = The
content.itemB: The internet, The sky, The environment
content.itemD: Say to the user to write a phrase using those articles

title: Nouns
description: The use of nouns in the English language.

content.itemA: A noun is a word that functions as the name of a specific object or set of objects, such as living creatures, places, actions, qualities, states of existence, or ideas.
content.itemB: Person, Place, Thing, Idea

title: Verbs

title: Adjectives
description: The use of adjectives in the English language.

content.itemA: An adjective is a word that describes or modifies a noun or pronoun. It provides more information about a person, place, or thing.
content.itemB: Quality, Size, Age, Color, Origin
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him 1 question about a person, place, or thing.

content.itemA: Qualitative adjectives describe the qualities, characteristics, or properties of a noun (color, size, texture, or opinion). They answer the question 'What kind?'.
content.itemB: The **blue** car; A **large** studio; That **expensive** microphone; A **clear** recording.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Qualitative adjectives.

content.itemA: Quantitative adjectives indicate the number or amount of a noun. They answer 'How much?' (uncountable) or 'How many?' (countable).
content.itemB: I have **some** money; There are **many** plugins; He has **little** time; We need **more** tracks.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Quantitative adjectives.

content.itemA: Proper adjectives are formed from proper nouns (names of people, places, or languages). In English, they must always begin with a capital letter.
content.itemB: The **Costa Rican** culture; A **Japanese** synthesizer; **English** literature; **German** engineering.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Proper adjectives.

content.itemA: Demonstrative adjectives point to specific nouns in relation to the speaker's position in space or time (near vs. far, singular vs. plural).
content.itemB: **This** cable (near/singular); **That** screen (far/singular); **These** speakers (near/plural); **Those** knobs (far/plural).
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Demonstrative adjectives.

content.itemA: Possessive adjectives show ownership or relationship. They always come before the noun they modify and do not use apostrophes.
content.itemB: **My** project; **Our** band; **Its** frequency response; **Their** instruments.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Possessive adjectives.

content.itemA: Interrogative adjectives are used with nouns to ask questions. The most common are 'which', 'what', and 'whose'.
content.itemB: **Which** DAW do you use? **What** song is playing? **Whose** headphones are these?
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Interrogative adjectives.

content.itemA: Compound adjectives consist of two or more words working as one. They are usually hyphenated when they appear before the noun they modify.
content.itemB: A **well-known** artist; A **high-performance** PC; A **five-minute** break; A **first-class** recording.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Compound adjectives.

content.itemA: Distributive adjectives refer to specific members of a group individually. They are usually followed by a singular noun to emphasize each element separately.
content.itemB: **Each** student; **Every** track; **Either** option is fine; **Neither** plugin worked.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Distributive adjectives.

content.itemA: Indefinite adjectives provide non-specific information about the quantity or identity of a noun. They describe 'some' but not 'exactly how many'.
content.itemB: **Some** people; **Many** ideas; **Few** mistakes; **Several** tracks; **Any** DAW.
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Indefinite adjectives.

content.itemA: Participial adjectives ending in '-ed' describe a feeling or emotion (how someone feels), while those ending in '-ing' describe the thing causing the feeling.
content.itemB: I am **interested** in music (feeling); The music is **interesting** (characteristic).
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Participial adjectives.

content.itemA: Numerical adjectives specify the exact number (Cardinal) or the specific order (Ordinal) of nouns.
content.itemB: **Three** speakers (Cardinal); The **first** take (Ordinal); **Double** check (Multiplicative).
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about Numerical adjectives.

content.itemA: Approximation to all the common adjectives from the Oxford 5000 list only.
content.itemB: Remember what really is important is that you never surrender when things get complicated! So do it!. Here we GO!
content.itemC: Now let's see all of the adjectives. Don't panic!
content.itemD: Ask him 1 question about a some long period of time he have passed.

title: Adverbs
description: The use of adverbs in the english language.

content.itemA: An adverb is a word that modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb. It tells how, when, where, or to what extent something happens.
content.itemB: Manner, Time, Place, Degree, Frequency
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him 1 question about Manner, Time, Place, Degree or Frequency.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Frequency describe how often an action occurs. They usually go before the main verb but after the verb 'to be'.
content.itemB: I **always** record in the morning; He is **often** late; We **never** use that plugin; They **seldom** practice.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Frequency.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Manner describe how an action is performed. Most are formed by adding '-ly' to an adjective and usually follow the main verb or object.
content.itemB: He plays the guitar **beautifully**; Please speak **clearly**; She ran **quickly**; The engine works **smoothly**.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Manner.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Time indicate when an action happens, for how long, or how soon. They are typically placed at the end of a sentence.
content.itemB: I will call you **tomorrow**; We are recording **now**; He arrived **late**; They have been here **since** Monday.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Time.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Place tell us where the action happens. They do not usually modify adjectives or other adverbs, only verbs.
content.itemB: Put the microphone **there**; He is waiting **outside**; They traveled **abroad**; Look **up**.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Place.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Degree (or Intensity) specify the intensity or extent of an adjective, verb, or another adverb. They answer 'To what extent?'.
content.itemB: The mix is **extremely** loud; I **completely** agree; It is **too** quiet; She is **very** talented.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Degree.

content.itemA: Adverbs of Certainty express how sure we are about an action or event. They usually go before the main verb.
content.itemB: He will **probably** win; I **certainly** remember you; They are **definitely** coming; It is **surely** true.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Adverbs of Certainty.

content.itemA: Conjunctive Adverbs (or Transitions) connect two independent clauses or sentences. They require a semicolon or a period before them.
content.itemB: The PC is fast; **however**, it is expensive. I was tired; **therefore**, I went to bed.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Conjunctive Adverbs.

content.itemA: Interrogative Adverbs are used at the beginning of a sentence to ask a question related to time, place, manner, or reason.
content.itemB: **Why** did you leave? **Where** is the studio? **How** do you open this? **When** is the exam?
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Interrogative Adverbs.

content.itemA: Relative Adverbs introduce relative clauses and link them to a noun. The three main ones are 'where', 'when', and 'why'.
content.itemB: The studio **where** I work; The day **when** we met; The reason **why** I called.
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about Relative Adverbs.

content.itemA: Approximation to all the common adverbs from the Oxford 5000 list only.
content.itemB: Remember what really is important is that you never surrender when things get complicated! So do it!. Here we GO!
content.itemC: Now let's see all of the adverbs. Don't panic!
content.itemD: ask him 1 question about a long period of time in his life.

content.itemB: He thinks visionarily, it is visually stunning, it is vitally important, he spoke vitriolically, and she laughed vivaciously.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: The contract sat voidly, the market moved volatilely, it erupted volcanically, he acted volitionally, and he spoke volubly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He spoke vulgarly, she stood vulnerably, he grinned waggishly, she cried wailingly, and he stood waitingly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He looked warningly, it was warrantably done, he acted warrantedly, they lived warringly, and he moved warily.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He acted waywardly, he answered weakly, they lived wealthily, he sat wearily, and the task ended wearyingly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: You are welcome, he did well, the wind is westerly, they moved westward, and the ship sailed westwards.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He moved whiskingly, she spoke whisperingly, he walked whistlingl, it is painted white, and I am wholly convinced.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He acted wilfully, she agreed willingly, she moved willowily, he smiled wily, and he looked wincedly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: She smiled winsomely, it felt winterly cold, the air was wintery, he acted wisely, and she looked wishfully.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He left without a word, he spoke witlessly, he did it wittingly, he replied wittily, and he worked wizardly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: It was wondrously made, it smelled woodily, he spoke wooingly, he writes wordily, and they stood wordlessly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: It is getting worse, they bowed worshipfully, the worst is over, he lived worthily, and it was worthlessly discarded.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: You are wrong, he was wrongfully accused, it was wrongly timed, it was wroughtly designed, and he smiled wryly.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: She looked yearningly, it shone yellowly, the dog barked yelpingly, yes I will, and I saw him yesterday.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

content.itemB: He worked zestfully, she spoke zestily, the road goes zigzag, it was coated zinc-ly, and he spoke zionistically.
content.itemD: Say to the user to use these words by creating a phrase, and that try to practice with all of them

title: Pronouns
description: Master how to replace nouns to make your sentences flow better.

content.itemA: A pronoun is a word used in place of a noun. It prevents the constant repetition of names and subjects in a sentence.
content.itemB: I saw David, and **he** was happy; The piano is loud, but **it** sounds great.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write a phrase using those examples.

content.itemA: Subject Pronouns perform the action in a sentence, while Object Pronouns receive the action.
content.itemB: **I** love music / Music loves **me**; **They** called **us**; **She** saw **him**.
content.itemD: Ask him to write a phrase using Subject Pronouns.

content.itemA: Possessive Pronouns show ownership and stand alone (unlike possessive adjectives which need a noun).
content.itemB: This guitar is **mine**; That DAW is **yours**; The victory was **ours**.
content.itemD: Ask him to write a phrase using Possessive Pronouns.

content.itemA: Reflexive Pronouns are used when the subject and the object of the verb are the same person or thing.
content.itemB: I recorded **myself**; The app updates **itself**; We challenge **ourselves**.
content.itemD: Ask him to write a phrase using Reflexive Pronouns.

content.itemA: Relative Pronouns connect a clause or phrase to a noun or pronoun. They provide more detail without starting a new sentence.
content.itemB: The artist **who** won; The code **that** failed; The studio **where** we work.
content.itemD: Ask him to write a phrase using Relative Pronouns.

content.itemA: Indefinite Pronouns refer to people or things without being specific about exactly who or what they are.
content.itemB: **Someone** is here; **Everything** is ready; **Nobody** knows; **Anyone** can code.
content.itemD: Ask him to write a phrase using Indefinite Pronouns.

content.itemB: I did it myself; It belongs to me; The choice is mine.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using I / Me / Mine / Myself.

content.itemB: You should help yourself; This seat is yours; I see you.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using You / You / Yours / Yourself.

content.itemB: He fixed it himself; I know him; The car is his.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using He / Him / His / Himself.

content.itemB: She taught herself; We like her; The idea was hers.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using She / Her / Hers / Herself.

content.itemB: It works by itself; I found it; Its color is red.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using It / It / Its / Itself.

content.itemB: We built it ourselves; Join us; The studio is ours.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using We / Us / Ours / Ourselves.

content.itemB: They did it themselves; Call them; The house is theirs.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using They / Them / Theirs / Themselves.

title: Prepositions
description: Learn to describe relationships in time, space, and direction.

content.itemA: A preposition is a word that shows the relationship between a noun (or pronoun) and other parts of the sentence.
content.itemB: The cat is **under** the table; We will meet **at** noon; She traveled **by** train.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using under, at or by.

content.itemA: Prepositions of Place describe where something is located. 'In' is for enclosed spaces, 'On' for surfaces, and 'At' for specific points.
content.itemB: The file is **in** the folder; The laptop is **on** the desk; I am **at** the studio.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Prepositions of Place.

content.itemA: Prepositions of Time indicate when something happens. 'At' is for precise times, 'In' for months/years, and 'On' for days/dates.
content.itemB: We start **at** 8:00; My birthday is **in** June; The meeting is **on** Friday.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Prepositions of Time.

content.itemA: Prepositions of Movement show direction or the path of an action from one place to another.
content.itemB: He walked **into** the room; They ran **across** the street; The plane flew **over** the city.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Prepositions of Movement.

content.itemA: Prepositions of Agent or Instrument show who did something or what was used to do it.
content.itemB: A song **by** 2Pac; Written **with** a pen; Fixed **by** a developer.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Prepositions of Agent.

content.itemB: The cloud is **above** us; The temperature is **below** zero; Hide **under** the bed.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Above, Below, Under, Over.

content.itemB: Sit **between** us; One **among** many; Stand **next to** the speaker.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Between, Among, Next to, Beside.

content.itemB: Check **before** recording; Rest **after** work; Focus **during** the take.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Before, After, During, Until.

title: Conjunctions
description: The 'glue' of English syntax. Connect words, phrases, and clauses.

content.itemA: A conjunction is a word used to connect words, phrases, or clauses. They show the logical link between ideas.
content.itemB: Coordinating, Subordinating, Correlative
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using connected words, phrases, or clauses.

content.itemA: Coordinating Conjunctions (FANBOYS) connect words or phrases of equal importance.
content.itemB: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Coordinating Conjunctions.

content.itemA: Subordinating Conjunctions join a dependent clause to an independent one, showing cause, time, or condition.
content.itemB: **Because** it rained; **Although** I am tired; **Since** we started.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Subordinating Conjunctions.

content.itemA: Correlative Conjunctions are pairs of words that work together to join sentence elements.
content.itemB: **Either** this **or** that; **Neither** him **nor** her; **Both** red **and** blue.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Correlative Conjunctions.

title: Interjections
description: Short bursts of emotion or reaction in conversation.

content.itemA: An interjection is a word or phrase used to express strong feeling or sudden emotion. They usually stand alone.
content.itemB: **Oh!** I didn't see you there; **Ouch!** That really hurt; **Hey,** wait for me!
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using strong feeling or sudden emotion.

content.itemA: Interjections of Greeting and Farewell are used to start or end a conversation or to get someone's attention.
content.itemB: **Hi!** How are you? **Hello,** is anyone here? **Bye!** See you later.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Interjections of Greeting.

content.itemA: Interjections of Surprise or Amazement express a reaction to something unexpected or impressive.
content.itemB: **Wow!** Look at that view; **Gosh,** I can't believe it; **Whoa,** slow down!
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Interjections of Surprise.

content.itemA: Interjections of Joy and Approval are used to celebrate success, happiness, or to agree with something.
content.itemB: **Yay!** We won the game; **Bravo!** Great performance; **Hurray,** it's the weekend!
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Interjections of Joy.

content.itemA: Interjections of Pain or Displeasure communicate physical pain or a negative reaction to something.
content.itemB: **Ouch!** I bit my tongue; **Ugh,** this tastes terrible; **Eww,** that's gross.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Interjections of Pain.

content.itemA: Interjections of Hesitation or Thinking are used when a speaker is unsure, needs time to think, or is pausing.
content.itemB: I think, **uhm,** maybe tomorrow; **Well,** I'm not sure; **Hmm,** let me see.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Interjections of Hesitation or Thinking.

content.itemB: **OMG!** Did you see that? **Wow,** that's a huge synth! **Gosh,** I'm tired.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Wow, Oh, My God, Gosh.

content.itemB: **Yay!** The mix is finished! **Bravo,** excellent take! **Hurray!**
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Yay, Hurray, Bravo, Whoop.

content.itemB: **Hmm,** maybe more reverb? **Well,** let's try again. **Uh-huh,** I agree.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Uh-huh, Hmm, Well, Er.

title: The Verb 'To Be'
description: Composition of Am, Is, Are, Was, Were, and Will be in all forms.

content.itemA: Use 'Am', 'Is', or 'Are' to describe states or identities in the present.
content.itemB: I am a developer, He is busy, They are ready.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using states or identities in the present

content.itemA: Use 'Was' or 'Were' to describe past states or conditions.
content.itemB: I was at home, We were tired, It wasn't cold.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using past states or conditions

content.itemA: Use 'Will be' to describe future states regardless of the subject.
content.itemB: I will be there, It won't be easy, Will you be late?
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using future states regardless of the subject

title: Demonstratives
description: Using This, That, These, and Those to identify objects.

content.itemB: This is my phone, That is a building.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using 'This' (near) and 'That' (far) for singular objects.

content.itemB: These are my keys, Those are the clouds.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'These' (near) and 'Those' (far) for plural objects.

title: Basic Sentence Structure (SVO)
description: The fundamental Subject + Verb + Object architecture.

content.itemA: The standard English word order where the doer comes first, then the action, then the receiver.
content.itemB: I (S) drink (V) coffee (O), David (S) writes (V) code (O).
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Basic Sentence Structure.

title: Present Simple
description: Expressing habits, routines, and permanent facts.

content.itemA: Use the base verb, adding 's' or 'es' only for He, She, or It.
content.itemB: I work every day, He works in Heredia, They play music.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using habits, routines, and permanent facts.

content.itemA: Use 'Do' or 'Does' for negative sentences and questions.
content.itemB: I don't know, Does she study? Do they stay?
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Do' or 'Does' for negative sentences and questions.

content.itemA: When using 'Does' or 'Doesn't', the main verb loses the 's' because the auxiliary already carries it.
content.itemB: He works -> Does he work? (NOT Does he works?) / She plays -> She doesn't play.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Does' or 'Doesn't'.

title: Present Continuous
description: Actions happening right now or temporary situations.

content.itemA: Combine 'To Be' with a verb ending in '-ing' to show current progress.
content.itemB: I am speaking, They are recording, She is calling.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Continuous.

title: Past Simple: Action Verbs
description: Master the difference between Regular (+ed) and Irregular (V2) verbs.

content.itemA: Most English verbs are 'Regular'. To move them to the past, simply add '-ed' to the base form.
content.itemB: Work -> Worked, Play -> Played, Listen -> Listened, Record -> Recorded.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Regular Verbs.

content.itemA: Irregular verbs do not follow the '-ed' rule. They have a unique 'V2' form that must be memorized.
content.itemB: Go -> Went, See -> Saw, Eat -> Ate, Buy -> Bought, Write -> Wrote, Do -> Did.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Irregular verbs.

content.itemA: Some common irregulars don't change at all, while others change completely.
content.itemB: Put -> Put, Cut -> Cut, Cost -> Cost vs. Go -> Went, Be -> Was/Were.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Irregular Verb Variations.

title: The '-ed' Pronunciation Engine
description: The three phonetic rules for regular past tense endings.

content.itemA: If the verb ends in a voiceless sound, the '-ed' sounds like 'T'.
content.itemB: Looked, Washed, Fixed, Cooked.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using The /t/ Sound.

content.itemA: If the verb ends in a voiced sound, the '-ed' sounds like 'D'.
content.itemB: Played, Cleaned, Called, Loved.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using The /d/ Sound.

content.itemA: If the verb ends in 'T' or 'D', the '-ed' sounds like 'ID'.
content.itemB: Wanted, Needed, Visited, Decided.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using The /id/ Sound.

title: Possessive 's
description: Showing ownership and relationships between nouns.

content.itemA: Add an apostrophe and 's' to the owner to show possession.
content.itemB: David's studio, The boss's office, My friend's car.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Noun Possession.

title: Present Perfect Simple
description: Experiences and actions with no specific time.

content.itemA: Use 'Have' (I, You, We, They) or 'Has' (He, She, It) plus the Past Participle (V3) for life experiences.
content.itemB: I have visited Colombia, She has finished the project, They have moved.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Life Experiences.

content.itemA: For Regular Verbs, the Past Participle (V3) is exactly the same as the Past Simple (V2), ending in '-ed'.
content.itemB: Work -> Worked (V2) -> Worked (V3) / Play -> Played (V2) -> Played (V3).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Perfect Simple with Regular Verbs.

content.itemA: For Irregular Verbs, the Past Participle (V3) often changes completely from the V2 form.
content.itemB: Go -> Went (V2) -> Gone (V3) / See -> Saw (V2) -> Seen (V3) / Do -> Did (V2) -> Done (V3).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Perfect Simple with Irregular Verbs.

content.itemA: Some Irregular V3 forms stay the same as V2, while others return to the base form or never change.
content.itemB: Buy -> Bought -> Bought / Come -> Came -> Come / Put -> Put -> Put.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Perfect Simple with Irregular Verbs that stay the same and that return to the base form or never change.

title: Present Perfect vs. Past Simple
description: Choosing between general experience and specific finished time.

content.itemA: Use Perfect for 'anytime in my life' and Simple for 'at a specific time'.
content.itemB: I have eaten sushi (ever) vs. I ate sushi yesterday.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Perfect vs. Past Simple.

title: Future Forms
description: Comparing Will and Going To for predictions and plans.

content.itemA: Use 'Will' for quick decisions and 'Going to' for pre-made plans.
content.itemB: I'll help you! (Will) vs. I am going to travel (Plan).
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Future Forms.

title: Modals (Part 1)
description: Expressing ability, permission, and advice.

content.itemA: Use 'Can' for ability and 'Should' for giving advice.
content.itemB: I can play guitar, You should study more.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Can' for ability and 'Should' for giving advice.

content.itemA: Modals are 'invariant'. They never take an 's' for third person (He, She, It) and are always followed by the base verb without 'to'.
content.itemB: He can code (NOT He cans code), She should wait (NOT She should to wait).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using modals showing that they are invariant.

content.itemA: To make a modal negative, add 'not' directly after the modal. Usually used as contractions.
content.itemB: I cannot / I can't, You should not / You shouldn't.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using negative modals.

title: Comparatives & Superlatives
description: Comparing nouns and identifying the extreme.

content.itemA: Use '-er' (Comparative) to compare two things and '-est' (Superlative) for the absolute top.
content.itemB: Faster than / The fastest, Stronger than / The strongest.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Comparatives & Superlatives.

content.itemA: For long adjectives (2+ syllables), use 'more' and 'most' instead of changing the ending.
content.itemB: More expensive than / The most expensive, More beautiful / The most beautiful.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using comparatives and superlatives with long adjectives (2+ syllables).

content.itemA: If an adjective ends in 'y', change the 'y' to 'i' before adding '-er' or '-est'.
content.itemB: Happy -> Happier / The Happiest, Easy -> Easier / The Easiest.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using comparatives and superlatives with adjectives endding in 'y'.

content.itemA: Some common adjectives change completely in their comparative and superlative forms.
content.itemB: Good -> Better -> The Best / Bad -> Worse -> The Worst.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using comparatives and superlatives with common adjectives that change completely in their comparative and superlative forms.

title: Quantifiers
description: Mastering Much, Many, Little, and Few for amounts.

content.itemA: Use 'Many' and 'Few' for objects you can count individually (plurals).
content.itemB: Many developers, Many songs, Few bugs, Few tracks.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Countable Amount Quantifiers.

content.itemA: Use 'Much' and 'Little' for mass nouns or concepts that cannot be counted (singular).
content.itemB: Much coffee, Much progress, Little money, Little time.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Uncountable Amount Quantifiers.

content.itemA: Adding 'A' before 'Few' or 'Little' changes the meaning from 'not enough' (negative) to 'some' (positive).
content.itemB: I have few friends (sad) vs. I have a few friends (happy). I have little money (poor) vs. I have a little money (some).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'A' before 'Few' or 'Little'.

content.itemA: Use 'A lot of' or 'Lots of' as a safe choice for both countable and uncountable nouns in informal English.
content.itemB: A lot of people (count), A lot of water (uncount).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'A lot of' or 'Lots of'.

title: State Verbs vs. Action Verbs
description: Identifying verbs that do not use the -ing form.

content.itemA: State Verbs describe conditions, opinions, or feelings that stay the same. They are almost never used in the Continuous (-ing) form.
content.itemB: I know the answer (NOT I am knowing), I believe you, This costs $10.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using State Verbs and Action Verbs.

content.itemA: Verbs of preference and emotion are 'States'. They represent a status, not a physical action.
content.itemB: I love this song, She wants a coffee, They hate the rain.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Verbs of preference and emotion.

content.itemA: Verbs related to the five senses or possession are also 'States'.
content.itemB: This soup tastes good, I hear a noise, I own a guitar (NOT I am owning).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Verbs related to the five senses or possession.

content.itemA: Action Verbs describe physical movements or processes. These can be used in both Simple and Continuous forms.
content.itemB: I run / I am running, He eats / He is eating, They record / They are recording.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Action Verbs.

content.itemA: Some verbs can be both, but the meaning changes. 'Think' as an opinion is a State; 'Think' as a mental process is an Action.
content.itemB: I think you are right (Opinion) vs. I am thinking about the project (Process).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Mixed' Verbs.

title: Past Continuous
description: Actions that were in progress at a specific moment in the past.

content.itemA: Use 'Was/Were + -ing' to describe the background or duration in the past.
content.itemB: I was working at 8 PM, They were sleeping when it happened.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Past Continuous.

title: The Passive Voice
description: Shifting focus to the object of the action.

content.itemA: In a Passive sentence, the object of the action becomes the subject of the sentence. Use 'Be + Past Participle' (V3) to form it.
content.itemB: Active: Davi (S) recorded the song (O). -> Passive: The song (S) was recorded (V) by Davi.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Passive Voice.

content.itemA: The verb 'To Be' must match the original tense of the active sentence (is/are for present, was/were for past).
content.itemB: Present: Coffee is grown. / Past: The email was sent. / Future: The project will be finished.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Tense Alignment matching the correct transform of the verb 'To Be'.

content.itemA: Use the Passive Voice when the person doing the action is unknown, obvious, or less important than the result.
content.itemB: The pyramid was built in 2560 BC. / My bike was stolen! / The room is cleaned every day.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Passive Voice.

content.itemA: If you want to mention who performed the action in a passive sentence, use the preposition 'by'.
content.itemB: The code was written by the lead developer. / 'Thriller' was sung by Michael Jackson.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Passive Voice with 'By'.

content.itemA: For the Continuous Passive, add 'being' between 'To Be' and the Past Participle.
content.itemB: Active: They are fixing the road. -> Passive: The road is being fixed.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Continuous Passive.

title: Conditionals (All Types)
description: Mastering logical 'If/Then' structures from facts to regrets.

content.itemA: Use 'If + Present Simple, Present Simple' for universal truths, scientific facts, or habits.
content.itemB: If you heat ice, it melts. / If I work late, I get tired.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Zero Conditional (Facts).

content.itemA: Use 'If + Present Simple, Will + Verb' for real possibilities and likely future results.
content.itemB: If it rains, I will stay home. / If I finish early, I'll call you.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 1st Conditional (Possibilities).

content.itemA: Use 'If + Past Simple, Would + Verb' for imaginary, hypothetical, or unlikely situations in the present/future.
content.itemB: If I won the lottery, I would buy a studio. / If I were you, I would go.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 2nd Conditional (Hypothetical).

content.itemA: Use 'If + Past Perfect, Would Have + Participle' to talk about past regrets or impossible changes to the past.
content.itemB: If I had studied more, I would have passed the test. / If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the flight.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 3rd Conditional (Past Regrets).

content.itemA: Use 'If + Past Perfect, Would + Verb' to show how a past action or change would affect a situation right now.
content.itemB: If I had moved to Barranquilla last year, I would be happy now.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Mixed Conditional (Past to Present).

content.itemA: In the 2nd Conditional, 'Were' is used for all subjects (I, He, She, It) in formal English.
content.itemB: If I were rich... / If she were here, she would help us.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using The 'Were' Exception on the conditionals that explains that in the 2nd Conditional, 'Were' is used for all subjects (I, He, She, It) in formal English.

title: Relative Clauses
description: Using Who, Which, That, and Whose to connect ideas.

content.itemA: Use 'Who' or 'That' when you are adding information about a person.
content.itemB: The developer who wrote the code is talented. / The woman that lives in Barranquilla is nice.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Relative Clauses.

content.itemA: Use 'Which' or 'That' when you are adding information about an object, animal, or concept.
content.itemB: The app which I built is fast. / The song that you recorded sounds professional.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Which' or 'That'.

content.itemA: Use 'Whose' to show possession or a relationship between two nouns, replacing 'his', 'her', or 'their'.
content.itemB: The man whose car was stolen. / The student whose grades are the best.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Whose'.

content.itemA: Use 'Where' to add information specifically about a location or a place.
content.itemB: This is the studio where I record. / Barranquilla is the city where she lives.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Where'.

content.itemA: If the information is essential to identify the noun, do not use commas. If it is just extra detail, use commas.
content.itemB: The car that I bought is blue (Defining). / My car, which I bought last year, is blue (Non-defining).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Defining vs Non-defining commas.

title: Gerunds vs. Infinitives
description: Mastering the Verb + Verb patterns (Ing vs To).

content.itemA: Use the Gerund (-ing) after verbs like: enjoy, finish, avoid, mind, suggest, and keep.
content.itemB: I enjoy recording. / She suggested moving to Barranquilla. / Keep working on the code.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Gerunds vs. Infinitives.

content.itemA: Use the Infinitive (to + verb) after verbs like: want, need, decide, plan, hope, and promise.
content.itemB: I want to study. / He decided to stay. / We plan to visit next month.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Verbs followed by Infinitives.

content.itemA: Always use the Gerund (-ing) immediately after a preposition (in, on, at, about, for, of, without).
content.itemB: I am interested in learning English. / Thank you for helping me. / He left without saying goodbye.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Prepositions + Gerunds.

content.itemA: Some verbs like 'start', 'begin', 'continue', and 'like' can take either form with no change in meaning.
content.itemB: It started raining. / It started to rain. / I like swimming. / I like to swim.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using verbs like 'start', 'begin', 'continue', and 'like' that can take either form with no change in meaning.

content.itemA: Some verbs like 'stop', 'remember', and 'forget' change their meaning completely depending on the form used.
content.itemB: I stopped smoking (quit) vs. I stopped to smoke (paused an action to smoke).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using verbs like 'stop', 'remember', and 'forget' that change their meaning completely depending on the form used.

content.itemA: When a verb acts as the subject of the sentence, it almost always uses the Gerund form.
content.itemB: Coding is fun. / Living in Costa Rica is expensive. / Recording music takes time.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a verb that acts as the subject of the sentence, and that in almost it always uses the Gerund form.

title: Modals (Part 2)
description: Advanced deduction, probability, and necessity.

content.itemA: Use 'Must' (90% sure it is), 'Might/May' (50% sure), and 'Can't' (90% sure it isn't) to deduce current situations.
content.itemB: He must be the boss. / It might be a bug in the code. / That can't be the right password.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Must' (90% sure it is), 'Might/May' (50% sure), and 'Can't' (90% sure it isn't) to deduce current situations.

content.itemA: To guess about the past, use 'Modal + Have + Past Participle (V3)'.
content.itemB: He must have forgotten. / She might have left already. / They can't have finished the project yet.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Past Deduction by guessing about the past, using 'Modal + Have + Past Participle'.

content.itemA: Use 'Should have + V3' to express regret or criticism about a past action that didn't happen.
content.itemB: I should have studied more. / You should have told me about the error.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Past Regrets (Should have).

content.itemA: Use 'Could have + V3' to talk about a past possibility that was available but did not actually happen.
content.itemB: I could have moved to Colombia last year, but I stayed here. / We could have won the game.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Past Possibilities (Could have).

content.itemA: Use 'Needn't have + V3' when someone did something that was actually unnecessary.
content.itemB: You needn't have washed the car; it's going to rain! / I needn't have worried.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Unnecessary Past Actions using 'Needn't have + V3' when someone did something that was actually unnecessary.

title: Phrasal Verbs
description: Essential multi-word verbs for professional and daily English.

content.itemA: A Phrasal Verb is a 'Verb + Preposition' that creates a new meaning different from the original verb.
content.itemB: Give (entregar) -> Give up (rendirse). Get (obtener) -> Get over (superar).
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Separable: You can put the object between the verb and the preposition. If the object is a pronoun (it, them, him), you MUST separate them.
content.itemB: Turn off the light / Turn the light off / Turn it off (NOT Turn off it).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Separable Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Inseparable: The verb and preposition must stay together. The object always comes after the preposition.
content.itemB: Look for my keys / Look for them (NOT Look them for). Run into a friend.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Inseparable Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Workplace Phrasal Verbs (Essential): Common verbs used in meetings and project management.
content.itemB: Set up (establish), Call off (cancel), Put off (postpone), Carry out (perform), Fill in (complete).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Workplace Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Communication Phrasal Verbs: Used when discussing ideas or reporting information.
content.itemB: Point out (highlight), Bring up (mention), Look into (investigate), Wrap up (finish), Go over (review).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Communication Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Technology & Development Phrasal Verbs: Specific to your field as a developer.
content.itemB: Log in (enter), Hack into (break in), Back up (save copy), Pull up (open file), Pop up (appear).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Technology & Development Phrasal Verbs.

content.itemA: Social & Daily Phrasal Verbs: Common verbs for life in the city or meeting friends.
content.itemB: Get along (friendly), Look forward to (excited), Pick up (collect), Drop off (deliver), Run out of (finish supply).
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Social & Daily Phrasal Verbs.

title: Present Perfect Continuous
description: Duration of an action from the past until now.

content.itemA: Use 'Have been + -ing' to emphasize how long an action has lasted.
content.itemB: I have been working for 3 hours, She has been waiting since noon.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Present Perfect Continuous.

title: Past Perfect
description: An action completed before another action in the past.

content.itemA: Use 'Had + Past Participle' to show which past action happened first.
content.itemB: The meeting had ended before I arrived, He had already eaten.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Past Perfect.

title: Reported Speech
description: Reporting what someone else said by shifting tenses.

content.itemA: Shift the original tense back (Present becomes Past) to report words.
content.itemB: He said: 'I am tired' -> He said he was tired.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Indirect Speech.

title: Inversion
description: Reversing the subject and auxiliary verb for formal emphasis.

content.itemA: Inversion happens when we move a negative or restrictive word to the beginning of the sentence to create drama or formal emphasis.
content.itemB: I have never seen such a view. -> Never have I seen such a view.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Defining Inversion.

content.itemA: The Mechanical Rule: When you start with a negative, the sentence follows a 'Question Structure' (Auxiliary + Subject + Verb).
content.itemB: Seldom does he go out. (NOT Seldom he goes out). / Rarely did they finish on time.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using the Question Pattern.

content.itemA: Use Inversion with frequency adverbs like: Never, Rarely, Seldom, Hardly, Scarcely, and Barely.
content.itemB: Hardly had I arrived when the phone rang. / Scarcely had we started when it rained.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Frequency Negatives using Inversion with frequency adverbs like: Never, Rarely, Seldom, Hardly, Scarcely, and Barely.

content.itemA: Use Inversion after expressions starting with 'Only' (Only then, Only later, Only after) and 'Not' (Not only, Not once).
content.itemB: Not only did he code the app, but he also mixed the song. / Only then did I realize the truth.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Restrictive Expressions using Inversion after expressions starting with 'Only' (Only then, Only later, Only after) and 'Not' (Not only, Not once).

content.itemA: In formal English, you can use Inversion for Conditionals by removing 'If' and starting with 'Should', 'Were', or 'Had'.
content.itemB: If you should need help -> Should you need help. / If I were you -> Were I you.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Conditional Inversion by removing 'If' and starting with 'Should', 'Were', or 'Had'.

content.itemA: Use 'No sooner... than' to describe two events that happen almost at the same time in the past.
content.itemB: No sooner had I sat down than the doorbell rang.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'No sooner... than' using Immediate Actions.

title: The Subjunctive Mood
description: Formal requests, suggestions, and requirements.

content.itemA: Use the base form of a verb after specific words like 'Suggest' or 'Vital'.
content.itemB: I suggest that he be present, It is vital that she stay.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Formal Requests using the base form of a verb after specific words like 'Suggest' or 'Vital'.

title: Causative Verbs
description: When someone else performs an action for you.

content.itemA: Use 'Have' or 'Get' + object + participle when someone does work for you.
content.itemB: I had my car fixed, I got my hair cut.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Service Logic using 'Have' or 'Get' + object + participle when someone does work for you.

title: Participle Clauses
description: Shortening sentences using -ing or -ed phrases.

content.itemA: Reduce a sentence by using a participle to explain the cause or time.
content.itemB: Feeling sick, he stayed home. Having finished, I left.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using Sentence Reduction by reducing a sentence by using a participle to explain the cause or time.

title: Advanced Linkers
description: High-level transitions for professional writing and formal speaking.

content.itemA: Use 'Albeit' (aunque) to add a contrasting fact. It is usually followed by an adjective or a short phrase, not a full sentence.
content.itemB: The project was successful, albeit expensive. / He accepted the job, albeit reluctantly.
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Albeit' to add a contrasting fact.

content.itemA: Use 'Notwithstanding' (a pesar de) as a formal version of 'despite'. It can be placed at the beginning or the end of the phrase.
content.itemB: Notwithstanding the technical bugs, the launch was a hit. / The bugs notwithstanding, we launched.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Notwithstanding' as a formal version of 'despite'.

content.itemA: Use 'Consequently', 'Hence', or 'Thus' to show a logical result. These are much more formal than 'so'.
content.itemB: The server crashed; consequently, all data was lost. / The budget was cut, hence the delay.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Consequently', 'Hence', or 'Thus' to show a logical result.

content.itemA: Use 'Furthermore' or 'Moreover' to add an important point that supports your previous argument.
content.itemB: The app is fast; furthermore, it is very secure. / Moreover, the new UI is user-friendly.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Furthermore' or 'Moreover' to add an important point that supports your previous argument.

content.itemA: Use 'Inasmuch as' to explain the reason or the extent to which something is true.
content.itemB: The plan was a success inasmuch as it reached the target audience.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Inasmuch as' to explain the reason or the extent to which something is true.

content.itemA: Use 'Conversely' to introduce an idea that is the direct opposite of the one just mentioned.
content.itemB: The north is cold; conversely, the south is extremely hot.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using 'Conversely' to introduce an idea that is the direct opposite of the one just mentioned.

title: Reading Immersion Intro
description: Transitioning from grammar to reading comprehension and literary analysis.

content.itemA: Welcome to the Reading Section. In the following levels, we will explore complex common topics and beautiful lyrics from famous authors. Your goal is to absorb the context, identify advanced vocabulary, and appreciate the rhythm of professional and creative English prose.
content.itemB: Immersion, Comprehension, Analysis, Prose, Literary
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using literature.

content.itemA: The modern metropolis is a sprawling labyrinth of concrete and steel, where the cacophony of sirens and rushing footsteps creates a relentless urban symphony. Skyscrapers pierce the firmament, standing as monuments to human ambition, yet beneath their shadows, the ephemeral nature of city life is palpable. Residents navigate through a myriad of narrow alleys and bustling thoroughfares, often feeling like cogs in a gargantuan machine. Despite the overwhelming congestion, there is an inherent beauty in the juxtaposition of historic brownstones and avant-garde glass towers, representing a confluence of past and future aspirations within a singular, vibrating space.
content.itemB: Cacophony, Firmament, Ephemeral, Palpable, Myriad, Thoroughfares, Gargantuan, Juxtaposition, Avant-garde, Confluence
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Urban Architecture & Life.

content.itemA: The rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence has sparked a contentious debate regarding the sanctity of human labor and creativity. While some view these neural networks as a panacea for global inefficiencies, others perceive them as an existential threat to individual agency. Large language models operate by parsing vast datasets to predict linguistic patterns, an achievement that is technically profound yet devoid of actual sentience. As we integrate these autonomous systems into the bedrock of our society, the line between human intuition and algorithmic precision becomes increasingly blurred, necessitating a robust ethical framework to prevent the inadvertent subversion of our fundamental democratic values.
content.itemB: Proliferation, Contentious, Sanctity, Panacea, Existential, Sentience, Bedrock, Intuition, Algorithmic, Inadvertent
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using The AI Revolution.

content.itemA: Call me Ishmael. Some years ago—never mind how long precisely—having little or no money in my purse, and nothing particular to interest me on shore, I thought I would sail about a little and see the watery part of the world. It is a way I have of driving off the spleen and regulating the circulation. Whenever I find myself growing grim about the mouth; whenever it is a damp, drizzly November in my soul; whenever I find myself involuntarily pausing before coffin warehouses, and bringing up the rear of every funeral I meet... then, I account it high time to get to sea as soon as I can.
content.itemB: Herman Melville
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using an animal story.

content.itemA: In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I've been turning over in my mind ever since. 'Whenever you feel like criticizing any one,' he told me, 'just remember that all the people in this world haven't had the advantages that you've had.' He didn't say any more, but we've always been unusually communicative in a reserved way, and I understood that he meant a great deal more than that. In consequence, I'm inclined to reserve all judgments, a habit that has opened up many curious natures to me and also made me the victim of not a few veteran bores.
content.itemB: F. Scott Fitzgerald
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using an inspirational human that changed the world.

content.itemA: The global climate crisis represents an unprecedented challenge to the delicate equilibrium of our planet's ecosystems. As temperatures fluctuate, we witness the accelerated melting of polar ice caps and the subsequent rise in sea levels, threatening coastal topographies. The repercussions extend beyond environmental degradation, impacting socio-economic stability and food security worldwide. Mitigation efforts require a multifaceted approach, involving a paradigm shift in how we consume energy and manage natural resources. To avert a cataclysmic outcome, international cooperation must transcend political boundaries, fostering a collective commitment to sustainability and the preservation of biodiversity for future generations.
content.itemB: Equilibrium, Ecosystems, Fluctuate, Topographies, Repercussions, Degradation, Multifaceted, Paradigm, Cataclysmic, Biodiversity
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using environment.

content.itemA: In the realm of psychology, the concept of cognitive dissonance explains the mental discomfort experienced by individuals who hold two or more contradictory beliefs or values. This psychological phenomenon often leads to a subconscious drive to resolve the inconsistency, either by altering one's perceptions or justifying the behavior. Such internal conflict can be quite profound, influencing decision-making processes and personal identity. Understanding the nuances of human behavior requires an acknowledgment of these intrinsic biases and the complex heuristics our brains employ to navigate a world saturated with information and conflicting social cues.
content.itemB: Cognitive, Dissonance, Contradictory, Phenomenon, Subconscious, Inconsistency, Nuances, Intrinsic, Biases, Heuristics
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using The Human Mind.

content.itemA: Economic globalization has facilitated an intricate web of interdependence among nations, where the prosperity of one is inextricably linked to the fiscal health of others. While this integration has spurred innovation and expanded market access, it has also exacerbated wealth inequality and localized industrial decline. The volatility of international markets means that a minor fluctuation in one corner of the globe can trigger a cascading effect across continents. Navigating this landscape requires astute policy-making and a nuanced understanding of trade dynamics, ensuring that the benefits of a globalized economy are distributed more equitably across diverse populations.
content.itemB: Globalization, Interdependence, Inextricably, Fiscal, Integration, Exacerbated, Volatility, Fluctuation, Astute, Equitably
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Global Economic Dynamics.

content.itemA: Whether I shall turn out to be the hero of my own life, or whether that station will be held by anybody else, these pages must show. To begin my life with the beginning of my life, I record that I was born (as I have been informed and believe) on a Friday, at twelve o'clock at night. It was remarked that the clock began to strike, and I began to cry, simultaneously. In consideration of the day and hour of my birth, it was declared by the nurse, and by some sage women in the neighborhood, first, that I was privileged to see ghosts and spirits; second, that I was destined to be unlucky in life.
content.itemB: Charles Dickens
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Artistic World.

content.itemA: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair, we had everything before us, we had nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were all going direct the other way—in short, the period was so far like the present period, that some of its noisiest authorities insisted on its being received, for good or for evil, in the superlative degree of comparison only.
content.itemB: Charles Dickens
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a tale.

content.itemA: The philosophy of existentialism posits that individuals are entirely free and responsible for their own development through acts of the will. This school of thought emphasizes the subjective experience of human existence, often highlighting the inherent angst and absurdity associated with a universe devoid of objective meaning. Prominent thinkers in this field argue that we must confront our own mortality and the vast, often indifferent void of the cosmos to truly define our essence. In essence, existence precedes essence; we are not born with a predefined purpose but must forge one through the choices we make and the values we choose to uphold.
content.itemB: Philosophy, Existentialism, Subjective, Absurdity, Devoid, Mortality, Indifferent, Cosmos, Precedes, Predefined
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Philosophy.

content.itemA: Biotechnology has reached a pivotal juncture where the ability to manipulate the genetic code of living organisms is no longer the stuff of science fiction. Techniques like CRISPR-Cas9 allow for unprecedented precision in gene editing, offering the potential to eradicate hereditary diseases and enhance agricultural yields. However, this scientific prowess is accompanied by profound ethical dilemmas regarding the 'design' of life and the long-term ecological consequences of altering natural selection. As we stand on the precipice of a new biological era, the international community must establish rigorous protocols to ensure that these powerful tools are utilized responsibly and for the greater good of humanity.
content.itemB: Biotechnology, Pivotal, Juncture, Manipulate, Unprecedented, Eradicate, Hereditary, Prowess, Precipice, Rigorous
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Genetics.

content.itemA: I have dreamt in my life, dreams that have stayed with me ever after, and changed my ideas; they have gone through and through me, like wine through water, and altered the colour of my mind. And this is one: I’m going to tell it – but take care not to smile at any part of it. If I should settle my whole life on a single hope, and that hope should fail, then I should be lost; and if I should find my heart's desire, and it should turn out to be a shadow, I should be more lost still.
content.itemB: Emily Brontë
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using dreams.

content.itemA: I am no bird; and no net ensnares me: I am a free human being with an independent will, which I now exert to leave you. Do you think, because I am poor, obscure, plain, and little, I am soulless and heartless? You think wrong!—I have as much soul as you,—and full as much heart! And if God had gifted me with some beauty and much wealth, I should have made it as hard for you to leave me, as it is now for me to leave you. I am not talking to you now through the medium of custom, conventionalities, nor even of mortal flesh: it is my spirit that addresses your spirit.
content.itemB: Charlotte Brontë
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a book.

content.itemA: I saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open; it breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs. How can I describe my emotions at this catastrophe, or how delineate the wretch whom with such infinite pains and care I had endeavoured to form? His limbs were in proportion, and I had selected his features as beautiful. Beautiful! Great God! His yellow skin scarcely covered the work of muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was of a lustrous black, and flowing; his teeth of a pearly whiteness; but these luxuriances only formed a more horrid contrast with his watery eyes.
content.itemB: Mary Shelley
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a terror story.

content.itemA: The concept of 'The Butterfly Effect' in chaos theory suggests that small, seemingly insignificant changes in initial conditions can result in vast, unpredictable differences in a later state. This metaphorical expression illustrates the non-linear nature of complex systems, such as global weather patterns or economic trends. Within such a framework, the flap of a butterfly's wings in Brazil could, through a series of interconnected atmospheric events, set off a tornado in Texas. This underscores the precariousness of deterministic predictions and the inherent fragility of the structures we often perceive as stable and linear.
content.itemB: Chaos, Insignificant, Unpredictable, Metaphorical, Non-linear, Interconnected, Atmospheric, Precariousness, Deterministic, Fragility
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Chaos Theory.

content.itemA: Astrophysics explores the fundamental laws governing the celestial bodies that populate the vast expanse of the universe. From the high-energy fusion occurring within the cores of stars to the gravitational singularities of black holes, the cosmos presents a landscape of extreme physical conditions. Dark matter and dark energy, though invisible and poorly understood, appear to constitute the majority of the universe's mass-energy content, influencing the expansion of space itself. Probing these mysteries requires sophisticated instrumentation and a willingness to challenge our most basic assumptions about the nature of time, light, and the very fabric of reality.
content.itemB: Astrophysics, Fundamental, Celestial, Expanse, Singularities, Constitute, Mysteries, Sophisticated, Instrumentation, Reality
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using Astrophysics.

content.itemA: I say to you today, my friends, so even though we face the difficulties of today and tomorrow, I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed: 'We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal.' I have a dream that one day on the red hills of Georgia, the sons of former slaves and the sons of former slave owners will be able to sit down together at the table of brotherhood. I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin but by the content of their character.
content.itemB: Martin Luther King Jr.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Martin Luther King Jr.

content.itemA: We shall go on to the end, we shall fight in France, we shall fight on the seas and oceans, we shall fight with growing confidence and growing strength in the air, we shall defend our Island, whatever the cost may be, we shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields and in the streets, we shall fight in the hills; we shall never surrender, and even if, which I do not for a moment believe, this Island or a large part of it were subjugated and starving, then our Empire beyond the seas, armed and guarded by the British Fleet, would carry on the struggle, until, in God’s good time, the New World, with all its power and might, steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the old.
content.itemB: Winston Churchill
content.itemC: We Shall Fight on the Beaches
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Winston Churchill.

content.itemA: Four score and seven years ago our fathers brought forth on this continent, a new nation, conceived in Liberty, and dedicated to the proposition that all men are created equal. Now we are engaged in a great civil war, testing whether that nation, or any nation so conceived and so dedicated, can long endure. We are met on a great battle-field of that war. We have come to dedicate a portion of that field, as a final resting place for those who here gave their lives that that nation might live. It is altogether fitting and proper that we should do this. But, in a larger sense, we can not dedicate—we can not consecrate—we can not hallow—this ground.
content.itemB: Abraham Lincoln
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Abraham Lincoln.

title: Voice of History: Famous Speeches
description: Analyzing powerful rhetoric and historical calls to action.

content.itemA: Welcome to the Speech Analysis section. Here, you will encounter the words that changed the world. Pay close attention to the 'Rhetoric'—the way these leaders use repetition, metaphor, and emotional appeals to inspire entire nations. These texts are dense with high-level vocabulary and formal sentence structures.
content.itemB: Rhetoric, Oratory, Persuasion, Legacy, Inflection
content.itemC: Intro: The Power of the Spoken Word
content.itemD: Greet the user then ask him to write and say a phrase using history heroes.

content.itemA: I say to you today, my friends, so even though we face the difficulties of today and tomorrow, I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed: 'We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal.' I have a dream that one day on the red hills of Georgia, the sons of former slaves and the sons of former slave owners will be able to sit down together at the table of brotherhood. I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin but by the content of their character.
content.itemB: Martin Luther King Jr.
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Martin Luther King Jr.

content.itemA: We shall go on to the end, we shall fight in France, we shall fight on the seas and oceans, we shall fight with growing confidence and growing strength in the air, we shall defend our Island, whatever the cost may be, we shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields and in the streets, we shall fight in the hills; we shall never surrender, and even if, which I do not for a moment believe, this Island or a large part of it were subjugated and starving, then our Empire beyond the seas, armed and guarded by the British Fleet, would carry on the struggle, until, in God’s good time, the New World, with all its power and might, steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the old.
content.itemB: Winston Churchill
content.itemC: We Shall Fight on the Beaches
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Winston Churchill.

content.itemA: Four score and seven years ago our fathers brought forth on this continent, a new nation, conceived in Liberty, and dedicated to the proposition that all men are created equal. Now we are engaged in a great civil war, testing whether that nation, or any nation so conceived and so dedicated, can long endure. We are met on a great battle-field of that war. We have come to dedicate a portion of that field, as a final resting place for those who here gave their lives that that nation might live. It is altogether fitting and proper that we should do this. But, in a larger sense, we can not dedicate—we can not consecrate—we can not hallow—this ground.
content.itemB: Abraham Lincoln
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Abraham Lincoln.

content.itemA: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears; I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him. The evil that men do lives after them; The rest is oft interred with their bones; So let it be with Caesar. The noble Brutus hath told you Caesar was ambitious: If it were so, it was a grievous fault, and grievously hath Caesar answer'd it. Here, under leave of Brutus and the rest—for Brutus is an honourable man; So are they all, all honourable men—come I to speak in Caesar's funeral. He was my friend, faithful and just to me: But Brutus says he was ambitious; And Brutus is an honourable man.
content.itemB: William Shakespeare (Mark Antony)
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like William Shakespeare (Mark Antony).

content.itemA: Yesterday, December 7th, 1941—a date which will live in infamy—the United States of America was suddenly and deliberately attacked by naval and air forces of the Empire of Japan. The United States was at peace with that nation and, at the solicitation of Japan, was still in conversation with its government and its emperor looking toward the maintenance of peace in the Pacific... The facts of yesterday and today speak for themselves. The people of the United States have already formed their opinions and well understand the implications to the very life and safety of our nation.
content.itemB: Franklin D. Roosevelt
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Franklin D. Roosevelt.

content.itemA: You made it! You have successfully navigated through some of the most influential and linguistically complex speeches in human history. By analyzing these texts, you aren't just learning English; you are learning how language can be used as a tool for change and a beacon of hope. Your progress is truly impressive—keep that same energy as we move into the next phase of your journey!
content.itemB: Excellent Work!, Historical Insight, Linguistic Mastery, Milestone Achieved
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Master Yip Man.

content.itemA: The foundation of every state is the education of its youth. We must ask ourselves what kind of citizens we wish to cultivate within our borders. It is not merely the accumulation of knowledge, but the development of virtue and the capacity for critical thought that ensures the longevity of a democracy. Without an enlightened populace, the structures of governance become hollow, vulnerable to the whims of demagogues. Therefore, let us commit ourselves to the intellectual and moral elevation of the next generation, for in their hands lies the very survival of our shared values and the prosperity of our collective future.
content.itemB: Diogenes
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Diogenes.

content.itemA: I am not mindful that I should be here this day, in this place, to speak of the rights of man. But I am mindful that the eyes of the world are upon us. We stand at a crossroads where the path of justice is often obscured by the shadows of tradition and prejudice. To demand equality is not to ask for a favor; it is to reclaim a birthright that has been systematically denied. We must have the courage to dismantle the barriers that divide us and the vision to see a world where every individual is judged by their deeds and their character, rather than the circumstances of their birth.
content.itemB: Frederick Douglass
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Frederick Douglass.

content.itemA: The world is very different now. For man holds in his mortal hands the power to abolish all forms of human poverty and all forms of human life. And yet the same revolutionary beliefs for which our forebears fought are still at issue around the globe—the belief that the rights of man come not from the generosity of the state but from the hand of God. We dare not forget today that we are the heirs of that first revolution. Let the word go forth from this time and place, to friend and foe alike, that the torch has been passed to a new generation of Americans.
content.itemB: John F. Kennedy
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like John F. Kennedy.

content.itemA: During my lifetime I have dedicated myself to this struggle of the African people. I have fought against white domination, and I have fought against black domination. I have cherished the ideal of a democratic and free society in which all persons live together in harmony and with equal opportunities. It is an ideal which I hope to live for and to achieve. But if needs be, it is an ideal for which I am prepared to die. We must realize that the road to freedom is long, and we must march together until the goal of true liberation is reached for every citizen.
content.itemB: Nelson Mandela
content.itemC: An Ideal for Which I am Prepared to Die
content.itemD: Ask him to write and say a phrase using a brave history figure like Nelson Mandela.

content.itemA: The question is: can we, as a species, overcome our innate tribalism to ensure our collective survival? We have reached a point in our technological evolution where our capacity for destruction far outpaces our wisdom in governance.
